Now all my being, my thoughts converged on the character I should have played. I called Mary, praying to her that I wanted to give me the script of the film, of which I repeated the scene repeated times. But, Holy God, why start from the end instead of from the beginning? It seemed to me something so absurd! Only then I understood all the meaning. Mary was with me: not even a moment was missing. When the script returned to her, she murmured: «Courage, young lady; Everything will be fine, it will see. "I don't know if it was the words of that good woman or those who, a few moments earlier, had addressed me Gasnier, but now I felt a security that I would not have been able to explain the causes. I thought that a miracle would come to my help. I hoped for it at that precise moment from Providence or from my good star: one of those stars that I had loved as a child so much. It seemed to me to navigate in a much strangest unreality than that in which my stars were born. I was taken in a kind of spell and now I had nothing left but let me transport to the drift from that dream that could be transformed into a wonderful, bright reality. But here is suddenly the sun reappeared: a shiny, powerful sun, and the director's voice came to my ear like a trumpet rim: «Ready everyone! Turn around! "

On the same night, with candlelight (I thought Angela already asleep) I got up from the cot, I broke the bottle of massage oil against the wall, helping me with a handkerchief so as not to make noise and with the biggest fragment I engraved the wrist twice Left: a sign of the cross that I would have brought for the rest of life. I didn't feel pain: I went back to the cot, my right hand stands eyes, the left abandoned on the floor for the blood to drain. It was then that I saw Angela get up, collect the same fragment and repeat my gesture. We didn't say anything. They were born in spite of those who put me to the world, I had lived against all the arid rules of the society at the time, and in spite of those who wanted to challenge my destiny I was leaving. [...] Those who try cold suicide and use rudimentary means to cut their veins, as I did, usually imagine one after that will come, not always coated in the other world. Think of a thread of blood that flows under the door, a cry of alarm, a gesture of pity in extremis, to a pitiful rescue that promises to all, willingly or nolent, would bring to consciousness the remorse of an early death. In mind that the thread of my blood, good blood, unreachable, stumbled upon the crack under the door, just this I was waiting for anxiety: that a surveillance subordinated was noticed. I was about to leave this world in spite of those who wanted to challenge my destiny. I was born in spite, and, in spite, I wanted to survive.

[...] With the money earned one million lire, Mommy, Maria and I moved to a larger apartment in via Balzani and I could finally redeem my sister's honor. My father, if ever for a moment there had been, had definitively disappeared from my life, I no longer tried to love him. Precisely for this reason it was not difficult to turn to Aida's cachet by obtaining a surname in return: for me an empty shell, for my sister salvation. Maria Villani became Maria Scicolone and was finally able to turn the page, return to school, start living. It is perhaps useless to dwell on the sequel to the sad story. However, I was saved. With my means, as I could, I had traveled our family history, to understand what as a child appeared too big to face. And I had come to my conclusions. Not all men were like Riccardo Scicolone, not all the events had to repeat themselves. At my side I wanted a different person, who could make me really happy. I still didn't know with certainty, but life soon would show me that I, my ideal man, had already met him.

In 1963 Bernardo Bertolucci proposed to play in the role of the protagonist in the first of the revolution, his second director film. Of course I accepted, but for me it was a psychodrama. Bernardo took my soul in that film: my character resembled me too much. And when one thing concerns you so directly, it may seem even repugnant. Only when I finished it, did I understand how beautiful that film was. Bernardo was my partner for almost five years. I had met him through Pier Paolo. I saw him for the first time in 1962, when he was shooting the dried command, his first film, with Pasolini's screenplay. [...] Bernardo was ten years less than me and was really a boy: I specialized in younger men, the only ones with whom he ever managed to have lasting relationships. So, such a difference in age was scandalous, but not for our circle of friends who from the beginning made the cheering for us. [...] My work often took me away from Rome and he reached me. The last time he was in Turin, where I recited in Cesare and Cleopatra by Bernard Shaw. Bernardo therefore came to see me, but shortly afterwards we left each other. He ended because of me, because I had the habit of destroying true bonds. Of the two, I was the immature. I resisted. I had a bad wedding behind her. The only relationship to which at the time I was able to remain faithful without effort was the one with the stage.

I see myself at fifteen (1944) to a high school desk (which I have not finished), with the fascists who enter the classy, ​​in silence they look at us one by one. Then they call me, they say my name and surname, and they take me to the principal's study. I don't know what color my face was, but I was afraid that everyone could hear the beat of my heart. I thought, now they take me to Villa sad. Villa sad was a house at the beginning of the road that led to my school, where everyone in the city knew it were interrogated and tortured the partisans. But I was not a partisan. I was nothing. "Don't worry," I said to myself, "don't worry ..." Then suddenly, at the first question I understood everything. And the heart down, to beat faster. Now the cuddly takes me and I go down. "Been to Enrico Mazzucchetti?" It shakes me the ranking.  "Yes." "Where?" "I do not know." Enrico, known as Bubi, was my fifteen love love: the first. "I haven't seen for a while." I knew he had gone to the partisans, but a few days before I had seen it, he had come under my house to give me a kiss and a ticket that I should have delivered to someone who, to be recognized, would have told me: how is Bubi? My God, what had happened? "At that time?" They were threatening. "I don't see him anymore ..." and I stay there, with his mouth open like a alocca. Once in the headquarters of the principal they rummaged me in my pocket. Then they let me go. My innocent air had convinced them. I don't remember anything else. I found myself in class with my head detached from the body and the sweaty hands. I was talking to myself by imagining my mother's voice: «You are an unconscious, you are unfortunate. If you know your father kills you, and it's good. " And doing the mark of the CRO I blessed the idea that I had had to tear the ticket in small pieces with the letter after learning it by heart, word for word. When one morning going to school I was contacted by a girl I had never seen, to her question, pronounced with great determination: "And how is Bubi?" I pulled a very deep breath, then I said in one breath what I had to say and I passed out. But how do you be so fragile and emotional? I could never have become a partisan; Not even a relay ...

In 1962, Marilyn Monroe committed suicide. He was thirty -six years old. That news exploded like a bomb in those years of sweet life, like the announcement of a divine curse, of a punishment launched on a woman too beautiful who had played too much with the desire of men ... I was interviewed by some journalists at regard. They asked me if I was afraid of that fate of actress. Why that comparison? It was absurd. We had nothing in common, Marylin and I. I did not feel involved in that death more than any other girl from my generation could be. But I understood the loss, the desperation of Marilyn. Here is a star who had been lost. But she had nothing to cling to! Not only did he miss the great love. The presence of a secretary, of a doctor, of passenger lovers, could nothing against solitude and suffering of childhood, the absence of the family. He had been thrown to the public without anyone who protected it, increasing his fragility. On the contrary, I felt very strong, and above all deeply normal. I owe it to dad, to mom, in Blanche, Bruno, Adrien, and Beppo, our wolf dog. My family gave me security and normal. At home, there was talk of my brothers' electronic studies, in the same way that the filming of my film was talked about. We all had dreams, desires, works. Adrien became Cameraman, but Blanche was unable to follow my footsteps in the cinema. It was difficult to impose another cardinal. In any case, this is what Cristaldi claimed. Of course, my job was a little strange, not only because I earned much more than all the others, but because it continued outside the set, during the evenings in a long dress, the seats by the photographer, the interviews. But I remained Claude, the older sister who had a minister's agenda, and that others treated like a star. The reporters came to interview me on this, on that, but in the family my opinion did not count more than the others. There was not even talk of the famous people I knew. It would have already been a lot if my mother had allowed them to call them "respectable people". They had too many money and were too famous. He pretended that they didn't exist. That slightly misunderstood world remained outside the home.

... Once upon a time there was a girl named Claude: a boy name. That name had also given her a boy's soul: from an early age he loved the challenge, cultivated physical and psychological courage, if he offended it he got angry, but did not cry. Then, one day, it happened that Claude looked in the mirror: and the mirror were the eyes of others, especially of the boys with whom he met at school or on the beach of Tunis. And in the mirror he realized he was a woman. Then he began to question himself: what is a woman? What am I, woman? How do I like a woman? I like a woman strong, active: I don't like the yielding ones, weak, tired, slaves of men. I like beautiful women: I am not ashamed, on the street, to turn to look at them. But their beauty, for me, is inevitably connected to their strength: and you can see, in the eyes, in the rice, in the way of walking. The strength is also the courage of simplicity: I do not like hypervestite, hypertruchy and conspicuously "lifati" women, because through all this they tell their weakness. I like the dignity of women: dignity to accept that the years pass without reacting, stealing clothes from the teenage daughters with the illusion of rejuvenating. Yes: women ... and I, how do I like it?

I took the bus every morning to go to the new school, this time I publish. I met Catherine Deneuve at the stop. She is bigger than me a few years old. Women look at each other much more than men, look at each other in a very primitive, almost animalcar way. Even more at that age when there is still no women, but you are no longer even girls. While turning into the future woman, you are perfectly aware of what is beautiful already physically and what are our defects. I looked at myself in all mirrors. I looked at myself in the eyes of others. I looked at me more in the eyes of other women than in those of men. For shyness and instinct; I already knew that man's eyes are a deforming mirror, even if I was still not familiar with the opposite sex. And then I didn't have the audacity that would allow me to look at a man in the eye. I was just looking for an answer that concerned me selfishly. I remember very well our scrutiny each other, as if we had intuited the mutual future, as if we were recognized; Two people belonging, even if in a very different way, to the same family, linked by a similar destiny. There was dismay in our looks. It was not a question of establishing who was the highest, who was the slimmer, who had bigger eyes, the thicker hair, the thinner ankles, how we dress, how we behaved. Each studied the other way of climbing the bus. We didn't go to the same school. We spied naively. The mirror, the mirror of Eric, always comes to mind. The mirror in Anthonny's way of being, my cousin. I was the mirror of Catherine, she was mine, and each through the other looked at herself. We did not express feelings towards each other, our gaze was only in search of an answer that concerned ourselves. Ours was a relationship of anxious, trepidant expectation. Each was waiting for the other, but not the other for the other; He waited for the other for himself. I didn't know her was also called Catherine.

Pavolini entered almost immediately, serious, his curious, stubborn and unhappy forehead under the very black hair which, with the mustache, interrupted the pallor of the face. With a courteous gesture he invited me to sit but I, vehemently, invested it with crowded words, the inflamed face, the eyes that were filled with tears [...] - goes there, excellence, she goes to that hospital to see with with His eyes the ignoble mockery to which those thousands of mutilated, of moribonds are exposed. [...] He looked at me for a long time as if to contemplate me intrigued, then, remaining sitting over there, almost on the ground, he said slowly, and his voice was beautiful, soft: - He is right. Thirty thousand lire (denarii that we have racimulated among all the people we knew) plus fear, and something on and in the eyes, which for months did not leave me. Of that hour spent in a kind of clinic, certainly not equipped for surgery, I remember the cold, the darkness that was out, was night, the indifference and tension of the doctor and the nurse. "Don't shout please, otherwise I don't operate it." There was fear in that room, their and mine. At that time for abortion it ended up in prison. In addition to that fear there was terror for the intervention I faced without knowing absolutely anything. The only thing I knew with certainty is that I would not have had anesthesia. For me, and all for me, there was also the weight of what I was doing. I was lying on the cold bed, I thought of my mother, and I really wanted to die. “If he is afraid he leaves. If he shouts, stop and the hunt away. " For years, that "La Package" has reversed me in the brain making me blush and curse my shyness. I haven't shouted. Pain. Shut up. I felt the tears slip through my hair ... not a lament came out. I looked at the doctor ... an indifferent piece of ice, who did his job by whistling under tone ... "He is a bad one" I thought. I certainly flooded with tears Dario, who was waiting for me in front of the closed door. He hugged me tight close. We were sick like two dogs who had lost the way home, moreover I felt so guilty, that I was sure that I would no longer have dared to look my mother in my eyes. Two thousand years of prejudices were the daily bread that many people ate. I, with my honestly Catholic Mother observant and convinced, I made indigestion of it. For Dario it was different. I met that doctor other times. We never even said goodbye. He became famous. Very rich. After the legalization of abortion, he also made conscientious objection. In words. In his studio he did all of Spello, nothing had changed in his abortic activity. Only the rate: one million.

In short, we worked in Cinecittà, that day, and nobody expected what happened. I was at makeup when I began to hear that there would be Mussolini's speech in Piazza Venezia, and that the workers had to go there. [...] Together [with Blasetti] we confused the dense and whispering crowd that had formed right in front of the second restaurant. "Second," he implied category, and that is the restaurant where the workers and technicians ate. [...] I looked around to see who was there: almost all the actors made up with the scenes on stage; Surrounded by Armigeri, Osvaldo, in his black costume by Cesare Borgia, Assia among the lace of his romantic character, Maria with the Ricci and the ribbons of Dorina in farewell youth, Alida in the busty dress of Manon, the little beautiful erect head, and many Actors, illustrious or not, surrounded by extras in the most varied customs: even Indians, dancers, Odalische Sowing under the stockings of veil. Everyone spanned carefree, as if they had been there for a normal stop of their ephemeral and hard work. Suddenly I saw the smile that Camerini was making me from afar, and I found myself immersed in a sudden silence: the radio had begun to convey the voice of Mussolini. At first nobody understood. That voice spoke briefly and was so dry and peremptory that no one, when silent, was able to believe that he had finished speaking, to explain. He was used to rivers of words, invectives, threats: nothing, never, had been more sinister than that silence, after those unheard of words. [...] The radio, always so urged to transmit the reactions of the crowd, could only record the vast silence that went up beyond, from Piazza Venezia, from that sea of ​​men who had also been chosen, called up, transported there alone because they applauded. [...] I looked around one by one the faces that surrounded me; In each, the same desert that was in the air without sound. On the face of Camerini, over there, hit by the sun, a amazed desolation showed. Blasetti had his eyes barred like an abused boy. Valenti died the upper lip and his face was hard, bad.

I remember when I had menstruation for the first time; I was thirteen, it was in summer, at the sea. I was not prepared. I knew what it was, but only vaguely. It happened in Villa Santiago on the French Riviera, and there were guests at home, collaborators of my father who worked with him at a script. [...] suddenly my father said aloud, turning to the party, who was a day of celebration because his daughter ... in short, she had become a woman .... she did not say it just so kindly, her were very raw words . It seemed to me an infamous betrayal, like those who steal your diary or those who say, as my sister did to our children's children, who put my wadding in the bra ... things that make you sink, who burn you forever. In his official announcement to the summer table there was cynicism, contempt for the woman who had become from that moment, with the duty to open my legs ... from that moment I had officially entered the pack of females to be mounted. Such a thing is not told to friends, at the table. I understood the curse of being a woman, woman with her recurring symbol: the blood, the open sex, injured, prey. By ridiculating the situation, asking for the blasphemous complicity of strangers guilty me, implying me in something dark, which I could not identify then. A feeling of dirt, anxiety mixed with the danger, remained for a long time: something that resurrecting the dormant animal in me.

My brother got married in February. Then I suddenly decided to get married too. In June. With Fabio Mauri, a childhood friend of Pier Paolo Pasolini. Pier Paolo was our witness. In a photo, which I certainly kept, brings a very elegant dress, of a shiny and bright fabric. Fabio, who was an artist, a painter, but he tried to convince me to abandon the theater. Curiously, despite my great insecurity, I resisted. When I married Fabio, there was a psychological accident in me: I believe above all because I didn't want to be a married woman, a wife. It was a mistake. [...] You can't escape. You can't really go away from yourself. But I didn't follow his advice. I flooded the apartment. I opened all the taps. The water reached the ankles. My mother -in -law took tea with friends. They heard the water go up to the calves. It is at that moment that he began to think that I had to be crazy. It is not normal to flood your apartment - especially in the presence of your husband's mother. My father had done the same year earlier, he had flooded everything in Milan's apartment, and had also built paper boats. You cannot imagine the rapidity of the water that goes up ... then I ended up divorcing.

It seems strange to say it but at that moment I did not understand what had just happened to me. Indeed, the first feeling I felt when I returned to my body was guilt. I thought back to the provocative dress I had worn, to the invitation I had accepted, to the champagne I had drunk ... it was my fault. And then that could not be rape, it was not so that Franca Rame had described him, the black chronicles in the newspapers that spoke of unexpected women caught by dark and beaten streets ... no, I had not been raped, because I still had on me The dress ... [...] I remember that the door slammed like an obsessed. My friend and I closed us in the closet fearing that he would throw the door down. Every time I participated in a festival, that it was Venice, Cannes, Toronto, Dauville, that monster showed up and I had to hide, helped by friends, body guards, publicists ... he presented himself to every first of any film, equipped with Paparazzi in order to take a photo embraced with me. At the time I did not understand why he did it, I thought he was interested in my work, or obsessed with me, then when I saw the smiling photographs that portrayed him with all the women who had accused him of rape, violence and sexual abuse, I understood that that of the Photo Together was an alibi who had been created over the years to defend himself: "They were all my friends" he would have told the trial. "All reports have been consenting."

It is April 25, the war is over. The partisans parade in Varese for the Empire square. Among them there are guys I know. They were of the first group of fighters who had formed on the mountains of the Cucia Val just after 8 September. They had perceived on Monte Grino and on the Cuvignone. The Germans had attacked them by shooting with heavy artillery, a real massacre, few had saved themselves through the galleries dug in the First World War; They were almost all inhabitants of the valley, the only ones who knew the path of those ancient walkways to the bearer. To celebrate liberation, a lot of people had reversed on the streets: at a certain point I see an armed group come forward from the bottom. With them there was a woman. He was the mother of one of the fallen of Monte Grino. Many recognized it and applauded it. Someone went to hug her. Suddenly she stopped and sat on a wall, nodding others to continue: the emotion was too big and no longer led to walking among the applause and cries. The day after the allies arrive. We had seen them in the afternoon pass through the city on the trucks. They had also arrived in my way. They threw chocolate and cigarettes to us. I blush at the thought of being thrown with others to try to collect something.  In the evening, in the courtyard of my house, great party. A gramophone, [...]. Then I look up, towards the dark window of the first floor: my house. More than seeing her, I understand it: my mother is there, she is looking at us. I know his thoughts, his torment: my brother, deported to a concentration camp in Germany, has not given news for over two years, in a moment I am close to her ashamed of my joy. I get tightened to her.

Rita Hayworth was shocking. He lived the worst tragedy for a star: a slow decline. Was he already sick of Alzheimer's when I met her? I ignore it. I just know he was deeply unhappy. One day, on the set of the circus, we saw her arrive with bruises on the face. I don't know who had reduced it that way. But I think he had many problems with their children who did not tolerate to see her drink. It was excruciating to meet a goddess reduced to that state. I felt almost uncomfortable, with my twenty -four years old, my smile, my successes. I wanted to hug her, console her. One day, we found ourselves together in front of the lights of the mirrors, for a touch -up between one scene and another. Looking at me he said to me: "I too was beautiful." A compliment that did not hide a certain hostility. I was unable to take myself and cried. My emotion touched it. In front of that blinding light, which does not forgive, we dropped the mask. I felt helpless. All you could do is surrender to fate, and perhaps, for a moment, accompany it along its last stretch of road. It was Vittorio [De Sica], once again, who guided me in this adventure: «Cesira is an all -round mother. He is humble, he has always worked and lives for his daughter. His approach to things is simple and direct. You have already tried all this on your skin, Sofì '. You know very well what we are talking about. You will recite without makeup, and without tricks. Be yourself, become your mother, and everything will be fine ». After so many years of Hollywood, the Ciociara brought me home to the hardness of my childhood. The war, which had been buried for a long time inside me, now returned out to give voice to this wounded woman, to her suffering, to her courage. I was thinking about Mommy, how he fought to defend us, to get water and food. At how those nights had to be heard in which the Moroccan soldiers camped in the entrance hall were drunk on our door. At how he was silent, without getting noticed, the young American marines who came to our living room to drink the brandy. Because he knew that the danger nestled everywhere, at any time, even where we believe it is safe. When Eleonora Brown presented me, the girl they had chosen to make Rosetta, I immediately felt responsible for her, for us. He had a shy and intelligent face and together he was waiting for us hard work. How could I become his mother? Suffer for her? How could I help her trust me? My instincts pushed me to look at her with the sweetness with which I felt looked at as a child, with the love from which I had been protected and caressed. And it worked.

Of course, staying in front of the camera was starting to like me, I had soon understood that it would be my best ally, however I detached myself too ease from my characters, letting the emotions assaulted me with waves, intense but short. That is to say letting yourself be investing, transforming, slavishing from one's role. And at the price of this abandonment that the actor gives life to a character. This new investment perhaps saved me from the tests I accumulated: there was no place for personal concerns on the set. An actor must be emotionally available. It cannot be subscribed to the problems, from its personal history. "Virgins" must be achieved and accept the adventure: let yourself be guided by the director on an unknown land, without fearing the danger. Photogenia was no longer enough for me. But I knew how to listen and I liked to work. Based on these two qualities I built a career that I had not chosen.

I know I am an Oscar actress for my unsurpassed interpretation in eight and 1/2 of Fellini. [...] Am I self -winning? No, nothing would bore me more. [...] Cinema has always been my second family. My daytime life took place [on the set] between trill and small thoughts, in a happy and carefree atmosphere. But at night, at night no ... on the return home my paralyzed mother awaited me, dying because of cancer. I comforted her as I could [...] But she asked me every day the same thing: to help her die. [...] In the morning the car came to take me to take me to the trick and, after the dressing, so on in the role of the disturbing, cheerful, very light Carla. [...] Then one evening my mother asked me: «Why don't you help me die? I would have done it for you ». Suddenly my selfishness, my lack of courage, the limits of my love, everything I owed to that extraordinary woman who was my mother were clear. And so I decided and I helped her swallow the hosts imbued with a deadly dust. [...] It was my last tribute for her. I was close to her, [...] aware that those were his last moments of life. [...] That's why I consider my participation in eight and 1/2 from Oscar.

The real paradox is that even Giuliana [Fiastri], Carlo's first wife [Ponti], wanted her freedom and, as a lawyer, studied the situation in search of a way out. Three times the bridges asked for the cancellation to the Sacra Rota, and three times it was denied them. The only solution to take away from us if nothing else from Bigamia's pity seemed to be the cancellation of the Mexican marriage, which however represented more a step back than a step forward. I suffered a lot, but in the bottom of my heart I had a clean consciousness and, however shocked by all the public and private accusations that rained on us, I knew I was right. I felt married, and this was enough for me, even if it was not always easy to live in the pillory, stamped with infamy. On the doors of the churches they were posted by the posters that put my films to the index and at the same time invited the faithful to pray for our slicing souls. We were submerged with often fierce letters, the worst of which came from a group of women of Pozzuoli. Feeling attacked by my city, from the heart of my childhood, of my land, caused me a deep wound, from which I started to recover a lot. Italy was divided between who was with us and who was against us. It was the Italy of Coppi and the White Lady, Rossellini and Bergman, a bigoted Italy that did not have a long future. Ten years more, and everything would have resolved with the referendum on divorce. But then we were still far away and we left only to suffer without making us annihilate.

Cristaldi had been a lawyer before becoming a producer. Dozens of pages organized my professional life, fixed the amount of my monthly salary, determined preliminarily and regardless of the importance of my cachet collected by the producers. Pages that established the names of the tailors who dressed myself, the expenses that Vides was charged, the following composed of a driver, secretary, press officer. My life was controlled. Step by step. With those articles, those paragraphs, those chapters on which I had placed my signature, I had sold my soul. The pact forced me to give up my free will. I was no longer entitled to my image. The Vides had bought me as a goods: color and length of the hair, measures, everything was written without me being able to affix even the smallest modification. Every decision concerning my personal life had to be taken in agreement with my producers. Cristaldi was good and generous, but it never failed to remind me that he had created me and that consequently I belonged to him. Even today, many years after our separation, many years after his death, I have not forgotten anything.

I want to be dressed in white and walk underground; I don't want to know what I wear nor what others wear, don't even imagine it; I will only warn the transparency with the tip of the fingers and, full of this feeling, I will feel good inside. I will touch something fresh under the feet. I know that there are the others near, men and women; They too in the absolute inability to see. Just feel, breathe. I don't know if this place has windows from which the sun could enter, or if it is completely dark. I think I would like more if it were dark, wrapped in a mauve, reassuring. A liquid, fresh and restful darkness. The nocturnal darkness of a moon -less summer night, without crickets. Then, with your hands, feel the others: touch an arm, with one foot touching another foot. Understand if I hear a man or woman, but not know who is absolutely. Imagine how it could be, or how I would like it to be, and decide whether or not to continue the approach according to the elements that I can capture. A dry scent of limoncello, the silky grain of the skin, just barely lukewarm fingers, breath of hay with a short breath ... it must be a cheerful tension, without fear of silence. It cannot be nervous. The wait will not have to know of sin, nor prohibited. Just a game that was born to become the altar of secret desire.

Returning to the homeland after so many triumphs the most bitter disappointment awaited us: we no longer knew where to go. For debts contracted due to the reconstruction of the Odescalchi Theater that had to be paid by the State and for the delays that occurred in the payment, the theater had to be abandoned to the dealer of Prince Odescalchi. [...] The war began from the day that the will and passion of eleven young people founded a stable theater in Rome. In fact, the Odescalchi theater was also called "theater of the Eleven", and they placed Luigi Pirandello in charge; Covered and insidious struggle by a society that has grabbed the whole monopoly of theater in Italy: theaters, repertoires, companies. In the face of this war, I [...] remained to fight next to Luigi Pirandello, then always exposed to all the revenge of the opponents, feeling that the cause for which it was fought was holy, and all in favor of us actors for the dignity of ours profession and for the honor of Italian theater. From the contracts that companies made with the Compagnia del Teatro d'Arte in Rome, directed by Luigi Pirandello, it was demanded that alongside the name of the Maestro it would imagine as with the diction of writing, unique and only, mine; [...] A company that in all the nations of Europe had been welcomed with all the honors of the highest theaters, was condemned to turn almost only in the province [...] the only hope of salvation, South America. She had been promised to American impresario that our company would have been alone starting that year, 1926, for Argentina.

Three years later Marcello would also disappear [Mastroianni ed.]. The last time I saw him was in Milan. He entered his car to go to the theater to try the last moons, by Furio Bordon. I still find it hard to return to the days of his death. The world spoke, declaimed, attended, celebrated. I was closed in my room, surrounded by my modesty. I didn't want to drain my pain to that of others, I didn't want to flag my feelings for the use and consumption of the media. I was alone, looking for a way to fully understand what had happened. On his funeral day I sent an orchid carpet, so that they accompanied him with their delicate freshness. And they brought him my love ever.

Going back to the years of my very early childhood, when there was war, I am increasingly convinced myself than in the horror and difficulties of that period I encountered two things, two certainties that a child is absolutely needed to live: protection And nourishment. It seems absurd to talk about one and the other when every day, now for now, we lived under the bombs and massacres and was hungry from morning to evening. But there, in Pozzuoli, where I was with my mother and grandparents, every day a ritual happened that managed to satisfy those two needs of certainty. Every evening he expected the last train to pass, and then all the people of the country poured into a long railway tunnel, where the next train would only go to the following dawn. We went inside loads of blankets and any rag that could keep us warm, they formed beds and threw themselves to sleep. A closed and heavy air, and so much frightened humanity. But in those hours under the tunnel we felt protected, and not only because it was believed to be bombs proof, but because we were all there, next to the others, to breathe the same air, to make courage, to even find the game and the joke. It seemed to me that they had purchased a huge family, my protective angels multiplied by ten, percent: safe and protected, with such a precise perception why I would have had in life again. Today I understand that that tunnel, after all, was for me the peace of the unborn child in the womb.
